8 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

After learning databases with PostgreSQL, I assumed search was simple.
If I want to search products, I can write:
SELECT * FROM products
WHERE name ILIKE '%laptop%'
OR description ILIKE '%laptop%';For a small dataset, this works.
If you have 5,000 products, users search for “laptop”, and the database returns results in milliseconds, life is good.
But then the system grows.
5,000 products become 5 million.
Users expect results instantly.
They make typos.
They expect relevant results.
They expect “laptop” to show MacBook, Dell, Lenovo, and HP laptops before laptop bags.
Suddenly, basic SQL pattern matching is not enough.
This is where full-text search engines like Elasticsearch come in.

The First-Principles Problem
Imagine an e-commerce website.
A user searches:
laptopThe backend must return products that are:

• Fast to fetch

• Relevant

• Ranked properly

• Tolerant of typos

• Searchable across title, description, category, brand, and reviews
A simple SQL query like this:
SELECT * FROM products
WHERE name ILIKE '%laptop%'
OR description ILIKE '%laptop%';has two major problems.

Problem 1: It Becomes Slow at Scale
The database may need to scan many rows.
For every product, it checks:
Does this name contain laptop?
Does this description contain laptop?With thousands of rows, this may be fine.
With millions of rows, this becomes expensive.
This is similar to a librarian searching every shelf, opening every book, and checking every page.
It is thorough, but slow.

Problem 2: It Does Not Understand Relevance
Suppose two products match the word “laptop”.
Product 1:
MacBook Pro LaptopProduct 2:
Laptop sleeve bagBoth contain “laptop”.
But if the user searches “laptop”, Product 1 is probably more relevant.
Basic SQL pattern matching does not naturally understand ranking.
It can find matches, but not necessarily the best matches.

Why Search Is Hard
Search is not just about matching words.
Good search must answer:
Which documents match?
Which matches are most relevant?
What if the user misspells the query?
What if the word appears in title vs description?
What if the query has multiple words?
How fast can we return results?That is why search engines exist.

The Librarian Analogy
Think of a normal database as a librarian.
If you ask:
Find books about machine learning.The librarian checks every book one by one.
Book 1:
Harry PotterNo match.
Book 2:
Game of ThronesNo match.
Book 3:
Introduction to Machine LearningMatch.
This works, but it gets slower as the library grows.
Now imagine a library with 100 million books.
Searching one by one becomes painful.

The Big Idea: Inverted Index
Full-text search engines solve this by flipping the problem.
Instead of:
Document → Search Termsthey build:
Search Term → DocumentsThis is called an inverted index.
Example:
machine → Book 1, Book 7, Book 12
learning → Book 1, Book 9, Book 20
database → Book 3, Book 8Now when the user searches:
machine learningthe search engine does not scan every book.
It looks up the words directly in the index.
That is much faster.

Why It Is Called an Inverted Index
A normal document looks like this:
Document 1 → machine, learning, ai
Document 2 → database, indexing, queryAn inverted index flips it:
machine → Document 1
learning → Document 1
database → Document 2
indexing → Document 2This inversion is the foundation of fast full-text search.

Elasticsearch
Elasticsearch is a distributed search engine built on top of Apache Lucene.
Apache Lucene provides the core search indexing and retrieval engine.
Elasticsearch adds:

• Distributed architecture

• REST APIs

• JSON documents

• Clustering

• Scaling

• Query DSL

• Aggregations

• Search relevance features
In simple terms:
Lucene is the powerful search engine under the hood. Elasticsearch makes it easier to use at scale.

Documents and Indexes in Elasticsearch
In relational databases, we think in terms of:
Tables
Rows
ColumnsIn Elasticsearch, we think in terms of:
Indexes
Documents
FieldsExample document:
{
"id": 1,
"title": "MacBook Pro",
"description": "Powerful laptop for developers",
"category": "electronics"
}An Elasticsearch index stores many such documents and builds search-friendly structures over them.

Why Elasticsearch Is Fast
Elasticsearch is fast because it does not search raw text from scratch every time.
It does expensive work upfront during indexing.
When data is inserted, Elasticsearch analyzes it, tokenizes it, normalizes it, and stores it in an inverted index.
Then search becomes fast.
This is a tradeoff:
More work while indexing
Less work while searchingFor search-heavy systems, this tradeoff is worth it.

Relevance Scoring
Search is not only about finding matches.
It is about ranking them.
Elasticsearch assigns each result a relevance score.
A higher score means the document is more relevant to the query.
Several things influence scoring.

1. Term Frequency
If the word “laptop” appears many times in a document, that document may be more relevant.
Example:
Laptop laptop laptopis probably more relevant than a page that mentions “laptop” once.

2. Document Frequency
If a word appears in almost every document, it may be less useful.
Example:
the
and
isThese words are common.
A rarer word can carry more meaning.

3. Field Importance
If a search term appears in the title, it may be more important than if it appears deep in the description.
Example:
Title: Machine Learning Fundamentalsis more relevant than:
Description: This book briefly mentions machine learning.This is called field boosting.

Field Boosting
Field boosting allows us to say:
title is more important than description
description is more important than bodyExample:
title: 3x importance
description: 2x importance
content: 1x importanceSo if “laptop” appears in the title, it gets a stronger relevance score.
This is extremely useful in product search.

BM25 Algorithm
Elasticsearch commonly uses a ranking algorithm called BM25.
You do not need to fully understand the math to use Elasticsearch.
But at a high level, BM25 considers:

• How often a term appears

• How rare the term is across documents

• How long the document is

• How fields are boosted
The purpose is simple:
Return the most relevant results first.

Typo Tolerance
Modern users make mistakes.
They type:
lapotpinstead of:
laptopA good search system should still understand the intent.
Elasticsearch supports fuzzy search, which helps handle typos.
This powers experiences like:
Did you mean laptop?or autocomplete-style suggestions.

Typeahead Search
Typeahead is when results appear while the user is typing.
Example:
User types:
macSuggestions:
MacBook Pro
Mac Mini
MacBook AirThis is common in:

• Google

• Amazon

• YouTube

• LinkedIn

• E-commerce search bars
Elasticsearch can support these experiences using analyzers, prefix queries, completion suggesters, and autocomplete strategies.

Elasticsearch vs PostgreSQL Search
PostgreSQL is excellent for relational data.
It can also do full-text search.
For many applications, PostgreSQL full-text search is enough.
But Elasticsearch becomes useful when you need:

• Large-scale search

• Relevance ranking

• Typo tolerance

• Autocomplete

• Search analytics

• Distributed search

• Log search

• Complex text queries

When PostgreSQL Search Is Enough
Use PostgreSQL search when:

• Dataset is small to medium

• Search is simple

• You already use Postgres

• You do not need advanced ranking

• Operational simplicity matters
Example:
Search users by name
Search posts by title
Search small product catalog
When Elasticsearch Makes Sense
Use Elasticsearch when:

• Search is a major product feature

• Dataset is large

• Relevance matters

• Users expect typo tolerance

• You need autocomplete

• You need fast search across multiple text fields

• You need log analytics or search dashboards
Example:
E-commerce search
Job search
Document search
Log search
Knowledge-base search
Elasticsearch and the ELK Stack
Elasticsearch is also commonly used for logs.
The famous ELK stack includes:
Elasticsearch
Logstash
KibanaElasticsearch stores and searches logs.
Logstash processes and ships logs.
Kibana visualizes them.
This is why many companies already have Elasticsearch running even before they use it for product search.

Basic Search Flow
A typical Elasticsearch search flow looks like this:
User enters query
↓
Backend receives query
↓
Backend sends search request to Elasticsearch
↓
Elasticsearch searches inverted index
↓
Results are scored and ranked
↓
Backend returns results
Database vs Elasticsearch Responsibility
A common mistake is thinking Elasticsearch replaces your database.
It usually does not.
In most systems:
PostgreSQL = Source of Truth
Elasticsearch = Search IndexYour database stores the reliable data.
Elasticsearch stores a searchable copy optimized for search.
If Elasticsearch loses data, you should be able to rebuild the index from the database.

Data Synchronization
If PostgreSQL is the source of truth and Elasticsearch is the search index, they must stay in sync.
Example:
Product updated in Postgres:
Product name changedElasticsearch index also needs updating.
Common approaches:

• Update both during write flow

• Use background jobs

• Use event queues

• Use change data capture

• Periodically reindex
This is an important system design challenge.

Example: Product Search
Suppose we store products like this:
{
"id": 101,
"name": "MacBook Pro 14 inch",
"description": "Powerful laptop for developers and creators",
"brand": "Apple",
"category": "laptops"
}A user searches:
laptopElasticsearch can search across:

• name

• description

• brand

• category
and rank results based on relevance.
A product with “laptop” in the name or category should rank higher than a random accessory that only mentions laptop in the description.

Example: Review Search
Imagine 50,000 product reviews.
Postgres query:
SELECT * FROM reviews
WHERE review ILIKE '%battery%';This may work, but can slow down as data grows.
Elasticsearch query can search the indexed text more efficiently and return ranked results faster.
This is why search engines become important at scale.

Important Search Concepts
Tokenization
Breaking text into smaller pieces.
Example:
Powerful gaming laptopbecomes:
powerful
gaming
laptop
Normalization
Making text consistent.
Example:
Laptop
LAPTOP
laptopall become:
laptop
Stop Words
Common words like:
the
is
andmay be ignored because they add little meaning.

Stemming
Reducing words to root forms.
Example:
running
runs
runnermay be connected to:
run
Analyzer
An analyzer defines how text is processed before indexing and searching.
It may include:

• Tokenization

• Lowercasing

• Stop word removal

• Stemming
Analyzers are one reason Elasticsearch is powerful.

Search Is Eventually Consistent
One important thing to know:
Elasticsearch is often eventually consistent.
If you update a product in the database, it may not instantly appear in Elasticsearch.
Usually the delay is small, but it exists.
This is acceptable for search use cases but not for critical transactional logic.
Example:
Do not use Elasticsearch as the final authority for payment status.
Use the database.

Common Mistakes
Using ILIKE for Everything
Good for small apps.
Painful for large search-heavy systems.

Treating Elasticsearch as Source of Truth
Bad idea.
Use your database as the source of truth.

Not Handling Sync Failures
If database update succeeds but Elasticsearch update fails, results become stale.
Use retries and background jobs.

Overusing Elasticsearch Too Early
If your app has 1,000 rows, PostgreSQL search may be enough.
Do not add operational complexity before you need it.

Ignoring Relevance
Fast search is not enough.
Search must return useful results.

Key Takeaways

• Basic SQL search works for small datasets.

• Pattern matching becomes slow at scale.

• Full-text search solves speed and relevance problems.

• Elasticsearch is built on
